meta.title: Marketing Ownership Risk Assessment
meta.scope_statement: This is an operations assessment, not a marketing strategy audit. It measures ownership, admin access, documentation, continuity, and governance.
meta.description: 20 operational questions to measure whether a small business actually owns and controls the systems its marketing runs on.

categories.label: Account Ownership
categories.description: Who actually owns the core marketing platforms.

categories.label: Admin Access
categories.description: Whether the business has direct administrative control of core systems.

categories.label: Documentation & Continuity
categories.description: Whether the business can understand and continue operations without vendor dependence.

categories.label: Permissions & Governance
categories.description: How access is assigned, reviewed, removed, and backed up.

questions.prompt: Is your domain registrar account owned by the business, not a freelancer, agency, or former employee?
questions.help_text: Examples: GoDaddy, Namecheap, Cloudflare Registrar.

labels.2: No, someone outside the business owns it

questions.prompt: Is the main website hosting account owned by the business?
questions.help_text: Examples: WP Engine, SiteGround, Bluehost, Squarespace, Shopify, Webflow hosting.

labels.2: No, someone outside the business owns it

questions.prompt: Does the business own the main Google account tied to your marketing tools?
questions.help_text: This may control GA4, Search Console, Business Profile, Tag Manager, or Ads.

labels.2: No, someone outside the business owns it

questions.prompt: Is your CRM or contact database account owned by the business?
questions.help_text: Examples: HubSpot, Salesforce, Pipedrive, Keap, Zoho CRM.

labels.2: No, someone outside the business owns it

questions.prompt: Is your email marketing platform owned by the business?
questions.help_text: Examples: Mailchimp, Klaviyo, MailerLite, ConvertKit, Constant Contact.

labels.2: No, someone outside the business owns it

questions.prompt: Does someone inside the business have full admin access to the domain registrar?
questions.help_text: This means they can change DNS, renew, transfer, and manage users.

labels.2: Only a vendor or outside person has full access
labels.3: Yes, someone inside the business has full access

questions.prompt: Does the business have full admin access to the website CMS or builder?
questions.help_text: Examples: WordPress, Shopify, Squarespace, Webflow.

labels.2: Only a vendor or outside person has full access
labels.3: Yes, someone inside the business has full access

questions.prompt: Does the business have GA4 admin access, including the ability to add or remove users?
questions.help_text: Viewer access does not count as admin access.

labels.1: Can look, but cannot manage it
labels.2: Only a vendor or outside person has admin access
labels.3: Yes, someone inside the business has admin access

questions.prompt: Does the business have verified owner or full-user access in Google Search Console?
questions.help_text: This checks if the business truly controls the property.

labels.1: Some access, but not clear control
labels.2: Only a vendor or outside person controls it

questions.prompt: Does the business have admin access to Google Business Profile?
questions.help_text: This is the listing shown in Google Maps and local search.

labels.1: Some access, but not clear control
labels.2: Only a vendor or outside person controls it

questions.prompt: Is there a current list of your marketing tools, owners, admins, and login recovery methods?
questions.help_text: A spreadsheet is fine if it is current and controlled by the business.

labels.1: There is a partial or old list
labels.2: Only a vendor has the full list
labels.3: Yes, the business has a current list

questions.prompt: Are your main integrations documented so the business can see how leads and data move between systems?
questions.help_text: Examples: forms to CRM, CRM to email platform, booking tool to calendar.

labels.2: Only a vendor has the details
labels.3: Yes, the business has clear notes

questions.prompt: Can the business quickly find brand assets, key logins, and operating documents without asking a vendor?
questions.help_text: Think logos, templates, SOPs, and recovery details.

labels.2: Usually only through a vendor
labels.3: Yes, they are stored in a business-controlled place

questions.prompt: Can the business export customer, lead, and email data from core platforms without a vendor?
questions.help_text: This checks whether the business can take its data with it.

labels.2: Only a vendor can do it
labels.3: Yes, someone inside the business can do it

questions.prompt: Could the business keep core marketing operations running for 30 days if the current marketing vendor disappeared tomorrow?
questions.help_text: Think website updates, email sends, lead handling, local visibility, and reporting.

labels.1: Maybe, but it would be very rough
labels.2: Mostly, but with important gaps

questions.prompt: Are key platforms set up under business-owned email addresses instead of personal or contractor emails?
questions.help_text: Best practice is using company-controlled email addresses.

labels.2: We are in the middle of changing this

questions.prompt: Do at least two trusted people inside the business have admin access to the most important platforms?
questions.help_text: Think domain, hosting, website, Google account, CRM, and email platform.

labels.2: Vendors still cover most of this

questions.prompt: Is there a written process to remove former employees, contractors, or vendors from systems they no longer need?
questions.help_text: This includes taking away access and updating records.

labels.2: Vendors manage their own access

questions.prompt: Does the business review platform ownership, admins, and permissions on a regular schedule?
questions.help_text: Quarterly or twice a year is usually enough for a small business.

labels.2: Sometimes, usually after a problem

questions.prompt: Does the business clearly know which systems would break first if a key vendor or freelancer vanished?
questions.help_text: This is about knowing the risk before a problem happens.

labels.2: Some of it is known, but not written down
labels.3: Yes, the business knows and has it documented

critical_conditions.label: Domain ownership is unclear or outside the business
Main Google ownership is unclear or outside the business
CRM ownership is unclear or outside the business
Email platform ownership is unclear or outside the business
The business lacks internal domain admin access
The business lacks internal GA4 admin access
There is no current tool inventory
The business would struggle if the current vendor disappeared
Critical tools do not have at least two internal admins
Ownership and permissions are not reviewed regularly

overall_actions.label: Start here
overall_actions.description: Top 3-5 actions that reduce operational dependency and ownership risk first.

overall_actions.label: Quick wins
overall_actions.description: Fast actions that improve ownership clarity, access, and documentation.

ui.intro_subtitle: This is an operations audit, not a strategy audit. It checks whether your business owns and controls the systems your marketing depends on.

completion_copy.severe: You are at severe ownership risk. Critical marketing systems may be controlled by someone outside the business.
completion_copy.high: You are at high ownership risk. Important systems exist, but control is still weak or unclear.
completion_copy.moderate: You are at moderate ownership risk. The basics are in place, but there are still important gaps.
completion_copy.controlled: You mostly control your core marketing operations. The next step is tightening documentation, backup access, and governance.
